As someone who's into rock climbing and photography, I'm used to taking risks but still needing clear facts; right now though, figuring out which posts are genuine feels like navigating dark cliffs without ropes sometimes.\n\nOne thing that helps me cope with this stress is blocking specific accounts known for spreading rumors or outright lies—my conscientiousness might be low overall by personality assessments standards, so I rely more on practical steps than deep analysis when dealing with problematic content. Sometimes just stepping away from screens helps reduce the anxiety associated with misinformation; I'm single so maybe no distractions needed like family support systems anyway.",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been struggling with all the fake stuff online, especially since I spend so much time scrolling through my feeds every day. It's like a constant battle against misinformation that can really mess with your head because of how high my neuroticism score is—it makes me anxious about what's real versus what's not. As someone who's into rock climbing and photography, I'm used to taking risks but still needing clear facts; right now though, figuring out which posts are genuine feels like navigating dark cliffs without ropes sometimes.

One thing that helps me cope with this stress is blocking specific accounts known for spreading rumors or outright lies—my conscientiousness might be low overall by personality assessments standards, so I rely more on practical steps than deep analysis when dealing with problematic content. Also following reliable news sources like Al Jazeera really grounds me since my religious beliefs as an Muslim mean there's extra weight around truthful reporting about global events.

I wish social media platforms made it easier for users who aren't experts—like me, a full-time student still learning these skills—to report fake stuff without feeling overwhelmed. Sometimes just stepping away from screens helps reduce the anxiety associated with misinformation; I'm single so maybe no distractions needed like family support systems anyway.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I started by acknowledging my personal context: age (19), high neuroticism making me more sensitive to stressors, and time on social media affecting exposure daily.

Then I considered how certain persona traits shape this issue—being a student with hobbies involving physical activities suggests an appreciation for clarity but limited expertise in digital literacy matters.

The psychological feature 'good mental health' actually complicates things because anxiety (high neuroticism) makes me more prone to distress from confusing information.
